meta.title: HPMOR Findings Map
meta.syncPolicy: This site mirrors ROADMAP.md by hand. If they disagree, ROADMAP.md wins.
summaryChips: The project is strongest when it exposes hidden assumptions, not when it merely restates folklore
A theorem that survives formalization is useful; a claim that changes under formalization is more useful
The page is optimized for readers who care what the math forced us to admit
The goal is not task tracking. The goal is epistemic damage assessment.

whyItMatters.title: This is not fan commentary
whyItMatters.description: The point is to stress-test rationalist claims from HPMOR and record where formalization preserved them, narrowed them, or broke them.

whyItMatters.title: The interesting output is not just proofs
whyItMatters.description: The interesting output is where the proof required an extra assumption, exposed a weaker model, or changed what the original argument seemed to imply.

whyItMatters.title: This is a prioritization tool
whyItMatters.description: For EA and LessWrong readers, the useful question is where additional formalization is most likely to reveal something non-obvious and decision-relevant.

keyTakeaways.title: Expected utility breaks under existential risk
keyTakeaways.change: Harry's stated framework works algebraically but self-defeats once every option carries some chance of negative-infinity catastrophe.
keyTakeaways.whyItMatters: The story gets the policy intuition right, but appears to need lexicographic preferences rather than ordinary expected utility.

keyTakeaways.title: Aumann does not apply to Harry and Draco as written
keyTakeaways.change: Agreement requires common priors, and the story's central negotiation does not plausibly satisfy that assumption.
keyTakeaways.whyItMatters: This sharply limits what 'rational agents must converge' can mean in-world.

keyTakeaways.title: Iterated prisoner's dilemma yields a non-obvious threshold result
keyTakeaways.change: The cooperation threshold depends on temptation and punishment, but not on the sucker payoff.
keyTakeaways.whyItMatters: That is the sort of thing the prose gestures toward but only the formal calculation reveals.

keyTakeaways.title: Some time-travel results are theorems; others are model warnings
keyTakeaways.change: Novikov-style periodic consistency is strong, but consistent-history propagation becomes fragile once multiple causes merge.
keyTakeaways.whyItMatters: The time-travel story is most credible when the site distinguishes general results from model-dependent constraints.

featuredFindings.title: Standard expected utility self-destructs under existential risk
featuredFindings.summary: Harry's stated expected-utility reasoning gives the right emotional emphasis but the wrong formal framework: if every action carries some chance of annihilation, standard EU collapses and cannot discriminate between them.
featuredFindings.whyItMatters: This is the cleanest example of formalization changing the theory rather than merely polishing it. The natural fix looks lexicographic, not ordinary expected utility.
featuredFindings.leanSnippet:
theorem eu_indiscriminate
    {p₁ p₂ : ℝ} (hp₁ : 0 < p₁) (hp₁' : p₁ < 1) (hp₂ : 0 < p₂) (hp₂' : p₂ < 1)
    (u₁ u₂ : EReal) :
    binary_eu p₁ u₁ ⊥ = binary_eu p₂ u₂ ⊥ := by
  rw [eu_bot_of_pos_prob hp₁ hp₁', eu_bot_of_pos_prob hp₂ hp₂']

theorem lexicographic_discriminates
    (a b : LexAction) (h : a.survivalProb < b.survivalProb) :
    a.lexLe b ∧ ¬ b.lexLe a := by
  constructor
  · left; exact h
  · intro hba
    cases hba with
    | inl hlt => linarith
    | inr heq => linarith [heq.1]

featuredFindings.title: TDT survives neither deflation nor coronation; it survives as an underdetermined intermediate theory
featuredFindings.summary: Formalization does not let TDT remain a vague aura of superiority. It shows both that TDT needs extra structure to define logical counterfactuals and that it still differs from EDT in the smoking lesion.
featuredFindings.whyItMatters: For EA/LW readers this is high-value because it upgrades the conversation from rhetoric about 'choosing the output of the algorithm' to an actual question about what extra machinery the theory requires.
featuredFindings.leanSnippet:
theorem logical_counterfactual_underdetermined
    {Action State : Type}
    (actual : Action) (other : Action) (_h : actual ≠ other)
    (trueProb : State → ℝ)
    (lcf₁ lcf₂ : LogicalCounterfactual Action State)
    (h₁ : lcf₁ actual actual = trueProb)
    (h₂ : lcf₂ actual actual = trueProb)
    (hdiff : lcf₁ actual other ≠ lcf₂ actual other) :
    lcf₁ actual actual = lcf₂ actual actual ∧
    lcf₁ actual other ≠ lcf₂ actual other := by
  exact ⟨by rw [h₁, h₂], hdiff⟩

theorem tdt_neq_edt_smoking_lesion :
    tdtExpectedUtility (smokingLesionProblem 10 100) (smokingTDTProb 0.5)
      smoke healthStates ≠
    edtExpectedUtility (smokingLesionProblem 10 100) (smokingEDTProb 0.9 0.1)
      smoke healthStates := by
  simp only [tdtExpectedUtility, edtExpectedUtility, smokingLesionProblem,
    smokingTDTProb, smokingEDTProb, healthStates,
    List.map, List.sum_cons, List.sum_nil]
  norm_num

featuredFindings.title: Sacred values are not irrational in general; they are only squeezed out on finite choice sets
featuredFindings.summary: Harry's anti-taboo-tradeoff line works on finite menus, but not in full generality. Lexicographic preferences can be rational and money-pump safe while refusing ordinary tradeoffs.
featuredFindings.whyItMatters: This is the sort of correction a rationalist audience should want: the original argument remains useful in practical bounded settings, but not as a universal philosophical law.
featuredFindings.leanSnippet:
theorem lex_rational : lexPref.isRational :=
  ⟨lex_complete, lex_transitive⟩

theorem lex_no_money_pump : ¬ hasMoneyPumpCycle lexPref :=
  rational_no_money_pump lexPref lex_rational

theorem finite_tradeoff_exists [Fintype α] [DecidableEq α]
    (r : PreferenceRelation α)
    (hdec : ∀ x y, Decidable (r.pref x y))
    (hr : r.isRational)
    (x y : α) (hxy : r.strictPref x y) :
    ∃ u : α → ℝ, u x > u y := by
  let filt : α → Finset α := fun a => @Finset.filter _ (fun z => r.pref a z) (hdec a) Finset.univ
  let u : α → ℝ := fun a => (filt a).card
  use u

legend.label: Solid
legend.description: A theorem-shaped result that is both mathematically non-trivial and recognizably faithful to the HPMOR claim.

legend.label: Qualified
legend.description: A useful result, but one whose interpretation depends on modeling choices, missing assumptions, or narrower scope than the prose suggests.

legend.label: Illustrative
legend.description: A machine-checked example or toy model that clarifies the shape of an argument without yet proving the general phenomenon.

legend.label: Tier
legend.description: The acceptance tier from the repo rubric: falsifiable first, faithful second, surprising third.

caseFiles.title: Time travel, regrettably, still has to be mathematically consistent
caseFiles.hpmorClaim: A deterministic time loop cannot produce arbitrary paradox; on a finite state space it must settle into a self-consistent cycle.
caseFiles.whatSurvived: The periodic-orbit theorem survived translation. The result is general, falsifiable, and recognizably the HPMOR claim rather than a decorative approximation of it.
caseFiles.whatRealityObjectedTo: Reality was cooperative but not indulgent. The current file still stops short of tight period bounds and a cleaner reduction to known fixed-point theory.
nextPressurePoints: Prove tight bounds on the minimal period
Identify the exact algebraic structure behind the result
Connect the theorem to standard fixed-point machinery

caseFiles.title: Drawing arrows from past to future is not the same as earning a causal theorem
caseFiles.status: Rebuilt with general acyclicity and topological ordering
caseFiles.proofStatus: General relation-based acyclicity and topological ordering proven; consistent histories currently best for forests
caseFiles.hpmorClaim: Acyclic causal structure should guarantee paradox-free histories.
caseFiles.whatSurvived: The stronger version now survives: acyclicity is handled via general relation-based machinery, topological ordering is genuinely proved, and the model says something structural rather than merely index-theoretic.
caseFiles.whatRealityObjectedTo: The remaining objection is narrower and more interesting: consistent history propagation is straightforward on forests, but merged causal chains need an explicit merge principle the story never states.
nextPressurePoints: Model acyclicity via transitive closure instead of the index order
Prove topological ordering exists in the genuinely non-trivial setting
Handle multi-cause nodes with explicit merge structure

caseFiles.title: The paradox story now cashes out as general fixed-point machinery
caseFiles.signal: Generalized beyond the original toy setup
caseFiles.status: General fixed-point theorems with HPMOR illustration preserved
caseFiles.proofStatus: General fixed-point theorems over arbitrary finite types; example retained at the bottom
caseFiles.hpmorClaim: The universe resolves paradoxes by selecting a self-consistent outcome outside the agent's intended options.
caseFiles.whatSurvived: The core paradox-resolution story now survives in a much less embarrassing form: the fixed-point results are stated over arbitrary finite types, not just one hand-built enum.
caseFiles.whatRealityObjectedTo: What still remains open is selection, not existence. Self-consistency may force some fixed point, but not necessarily the one a protagonist would find maximally informative.
nextPressurePoints: Generalize from a fixed enum to arbitrary finite transition systems
Characterize when fixed points exist and when they are unique
Replace the current storybook example with a structural theorem

caseFiles.title: Bayes survives contact with Lean, which is more than can be said for many speeches
caseFiles.proofStatus: Posterior convergence and monotonicity theorem family fully proven
caseFiles.hpmorClaim: Even an astronomically tiny prior can be overwhelmed by sufficiently strong evidence.
caseFiles.whatSurvived: The central update story survives cleanly. Evidence can, in fact, crush prior skepticism, and Lean has been kind enough not to object.
caseFiles.whatRealityObjectedTo: The slogan is no longer the interesting part. The real pressure point is quantitative: how fast does the posterior move, and exactly how much evidence buys how much confidence?
nextPressurePoints: Derive explicit convergence rates
Bound the evidence needed to hit a target posterior
Translate the theorem into quantitatively interpretable thresholds

caseFiles.title: Precommitment helps, but only under sharper structural conditions than the story admits
caseFiles.signal: Now substantive enough to bite back
caseFiles.status: Stackelberg, precommitment, and existential-risk results all landed
caseFiles.proofStatus: General strategic theorems proven; deeper equilibrium refinements still open
caseFiles.readingMode: Qualified theorem family with Tier-3 offshoots
caseFiles.hpmorClaim: Precommitment and strategic foresight can change what rational action looks like.
caseFiles.whatSurvived: The stronger strategic story now survives: leader advantage, precommitment value, and the existential-risk failure mode of standard expected utility are all formalized rather than merely announced.
caseFiles.whatRealityObjectedTo: The pressure point is no longer whether precommitment matters, but exactly when it helps. The latest roadmap finding is that its value can depend on equilibrium uniqueness in a way the informal argument omits.
nextPressurePoints: Formalize iterated prisoner's dilemma with discount factors
Prove subgame-perfect equilibrium results rather than rhetoric about credibility
Show precisely when restricting strategy sets improves equilibrium payoff

caseFiles.title: Game theory remains the best machine in the repo for extracting hidden assumptions
caseFiles.status: Aumann, Iterated PD, and FinalExam results all landed
caseFiles.proofStatus: Tier-3 style result cluster with explicit counterexamples and threshold theorems
caseFiles.readingMode: Faithful theorem family plus assumption audit
caseFiles.hpmorClaim: Rational agents negotiating under common knowledge and strategic pressure should converge or behave predictably.
caseFiles.whatSurvived: This is now the clearest example of the site's thesis. Agreement theorems, repeated games, and strategic confrontation all produce precise results once the assumptions are forced into the open.
caseFiles.whatRealityObjectedTo: The objections are now precise rather than speculative: common priors are not optional, threshold behavior matters, and strategic prose is still unusually vulnerable to sneaking in the wrong equilibrium concept.
nextPressurePoints: Aumann-style agreement results and their assumptions
Shapley or coalition analysis for the Harry-Draco-Hermione setting
Formal final-exam strategy analysis under incomplete information

caseFiles.title: Novikov self-consistency does not hand you an NP oracle for free
caseFiles.status: Multiple fixed points characterized; trivial loop obstruction proven
caseFiles.proofStatus: Selection obstruction formalized over arbitrary NP problems
caseFiles.hpmorClaim: A self-consistent time loop could be used to solve hard search problems by sending answers backward in time.
caseFiles.whatSurvived: The useful-loop idea is coherent enough to model, and self-consistency really does permit nontrivial fixed points when a solution exists.
caseFiles.whatRealityObjectedTo: The universe is not obliged to choose the interesting fixed point. The always-available 'do nothing' loop is just as self-consistent, so self-consistency alone does not yield computation.
nextPressurePoints: Investigate fixed-point selection principles beyond mere consistency
Compare Novikov-style selection with Deutsch-style CTC models
Characterize when nontrivial selectors can be justified physically rather than stipulated

caseFiles.title: Confirmation bias is not universally irrational; it depends on the prior over hypotheses
caseFiles.signal: Direct correction to a standard rationalist slogan
caseFiles.proofStatus: General prior-dependence theorem and HPMOR qualification result both proven
caseFiles.hpmorClaim: Testing positive examples is irrational; you should try to disconfirm rather than confirm.
caseFiles.whatSurvived: In Wason-style broad-rule settings, Harry's advice survives and negative tests are genuinely more informative.
caseFiles.whatRealityObjectedTo: The universal version fails. Under narrow-hypothesis priors, positive testing can be the Bayesian-optimal move.
nextPressurePoints: Characterize the exact prior families where positive testing dominates
Expose the crossover point between narrow-rule and broad-rule regimes
Relate the formal result to human experimental design under limited samples

caseFiles.title: Breaking energy conservation is not the same thing as breaking all of physics
caseFiles.signal: Sharp correction to a too-fast inference in the text
caseFiles.hpmorClaim: If magic violates conservation of energy, then magic is violating physics in a global sense.
caseFiles.whatSurvived: Harry is right that ordinary energy conservation can fail under magic.
caseFiles.whatRealityObjectedTo: The stronger leap does not survive. One broken conservation law does not imply the absence of all conserved quantities or all symmetry structure.
nextPressurePoints: Connect the discrete model more tightly to Noether-style reasoning
Ask what modified conserved quantity magic could preserve
Formalize minimal symmetry assumptions needed for alternate conservation

caseFiles.title: Perfect mind-shielding either requires perfect simulation or weaker security goals
caseFiles.proofStatus: Security hierarchy and perfect-simulation obstruction formalized
caseFiles.hpmorClaim: An Occlumens can simply clear their mind or hide their real state from a Legilimens.
caseFiles.whatSurvived: We can cleanly distinguish weaker computationally bounded protection from stronger information-theoretic protection.
caseFiles.whatRealityObjectedTo: Perfect shielding is much harsher than the prose suggests: if you want perfect indistinguishability, the fake state must match the real distribution exactly.
nextPressurePoints: Connect the model to zero-knowledge style guarantees
Study partial leakage rather than perfect concealment
Relate Legilimency power to computational hardness assumptions

caseFiles.title: Reputation makes dominance contests twice as fragile as naive intuition predicts
caseFiles.signal: Quantitative surprise with immediate narrative application
caseFiles.status: Reputation threshold and phase transition proven
caseFiles.hpmorClaim: Slytherin-style status competition can trap a group in systematically aggressive behavior.
caseFiles.whatSurvived: The model supports the broad intuition that reputation pushes the equilibrium toward more aggression.
caseFiles.whatRealityObjectedTo: The stronger result is quantitative and non-obvious: reputation helps hawks twice, halving the stability margin and making all-Hawk collapse arrive earlier than naive reasoning predicts.
nextPressurePoints: Extend to asymmetric-status players
Test whether hierarchy stabilizes or worsens the collapse
Compare with signal-ambiguity and coalition effects in the social landscape

hiddenAssumptions.title: Aumann needs common priors, not merely smart people talking to each other
hiddenAssumptions.description: Agreement-theorem reasoning is where reality becomes annoyingly specific. Bayesian convergence is not guaranteed without a shared prior, no matter how intelligent or impeccably Bayesian the participants may be.

hiddenAssumptions.title: Acyclic graphs alone do not tell you how to combine multiple causes
hiddenAssumptions.description: Once an event has more than one direct cause, someone has to say how those causes combine. Acyclicity alone does not perform this labor out of civic virtue.

hiddenAssumptions.title: Toy paradox resolutions can illustrate an idea without proving the general phenomenon
hiddenAssumptions.description: A hand-built example can show what a resolution looks like. It cannot, by itself, establish that the universe is obliged to resolve paradoxes that way in general.

hiddenAssumptions.title: Bayesian slogans are easier to prove qualitatively than quantitatively
hiddenAssumptions.description: It is one thing to say evidence beats a tiny prior. It is another to say how much evidence is needed, how quickly the posterior moves, and whether Harry's dramatic confidence update was numerically justified.

hiddenAssumptions.title: Credible-threat talk requires an actual equilibrium concept
hiddenAssumptions.description: If a threat is supposed to be rational when the moment arrives, then someone eventually has to write down the equilibrium concept. Plain Nash equilibrium is usually too weak for the threats HPMOR likes to brandish.

hiddenAssumptions.title: Self-consistency does not select the useful fixed point
hiddenAssumptions.description: Time-travel computation needs more than the existence of a loop. The trivial loop is always available unless a separate selection principle rules it out.

hiddenAssumptions.title: Perfect shielding is much stronger than merely looking calm
hiddenAssumptions.description: Occlumency only becomes information-theoretically secure if the fake state is distributed exactly like the real one; otherwise the best you can hope for is a weaker, bounded notion of deception.

hiddenAssumptions.title: The irrationality of confirmation bias depends on what hypotheses you thought were plausible in the first place
hiddenAssumptions.description: The anti-confirmation-bias lesson survives in the Wason setting, but not as a universal theorem. Priors over hypothesis space do real work.

hiddenAssumptions.title: Violating one symmetry is not the same thing as abolishing all structure
hiddenAssumptions.description: Magic can break energy conservation while preserving some other quantity. 'Violates physics' is too blunt unless the symmetry claim is specified.

nextAttacks.title: Expected utility under existential risk
nextAttacks.description: Clarify whether the story's policy intuition requires lexicographic preferences, bounded utilities, or a different framework altogether.

nextAttacks.title: Iterated prisoner's dilemma / folk theorem
nextAttacks.description: Push beyond the slogan that repetition helps and identify which structures actually sustain cooperation.

nextAttacks.title: Final exam strategy analysis
nextAttacks.description: Move from a threshold observation to a real incomplete-information analysis of the final confrontation.

nextAttacks.title: Conservation of magic
nextAttacks.description: Ask what symmetry assumptions are actually required for 'conservation of magic' to cash out mathematically.

nextAttacks.title: Interdict of Merlin
nextAttacks.description: Treat the Interdict as an information constraint and identify the quantitative assumptions needed for it to work.

nextAttacks.title: Precommitment changes the game
nextAttacks.description: Characterize the exact game structures under which restricting future options genuinely increases value.

tools.name: Aristotle (Harmonic)
tools.notes: Used on test claims and Bayesian posterior proofs. It has been genuinely useful, which is to say it occasionally produces code worth the effort of distrusting carefully.

tools.name: Manual + LLM assist
tools.notes: Used for Novikov, the grandfather paradox, and basic definitions. Productive, but still subject to the only policy worth having: every allegedly helpful artifact must survive Lean.
